content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 2, 1976

Mr. Forest Greber
Deputy County Counsel
1415 Truxtun Avenue
Bakersfield, California 93301

Dear Mr. Greber:

Thank you for your inquiry of July 1, 1976, in which you address several questions regarding the content and administration of Conflict of Interest Codes pursuant to Government Code §87300 et seq.

With respect to your first question concerned with client disclosure, please be advised that the Commission has recently passed Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18740 (copy attached). This Regulation provides for a limited privilege for not disclosing financial interests of attorneys. Thus, once it is determined that an attorney must disclose the name of a particular client pursuant to a disclosure requirement of a Conflict of Interest Code, the new Regulation would, in narrow circumstances, permit non-disclosure.

Your second question is addressed to the determination of actual reporting requirements. You ask whether a section in a Conflict of Interest Code which sets forth the contents of business entity reports might be drafted to permit the following language:

provided, however, that such designated employee need not list the name of every such person or persons if he files with his statement a declaration under penalty of perjury stating that to the best of his knowledge, information and belief all such persons not listed have neither had nor are likely to have any business relationship with the District other than to purchase utility services therefrom and that no decision made or participated in by such designated employee by virtue of his position will have an effect upon any such person not listed, distinguishable from its effect upon the public generally.

Mr. Forest Greber
Deputy County Counsel

- 2 -

August 2, 1976

In consideration of the model Conflict of Interest Code of
the Association of California Water Agencies, the Commission
specifically disavows the appropriateness of such language. If
it is not foreseeable that a client will be materially financially
affected by a decision of the District director, that director
need not report the name of that client. When, however, it is
foreseeable that such a client will be materially financially
affected by a decision of the District director, the clients'
names must be listed as a source of income to the public official.

You next ask whether the Board of Supervisors will review
or monitor the content of disclosure statements, presumably
those filed by designated employees within county agencies.
Generally, the answer is no. The Board of Supervisors will
not have any review or monitoring function regarding the content of disclosure statements. However, Government Code
§81005(g) as amended* provides that the Board of Supervisors,
as code reviewing body for heads of county agencies, will be
the recipient and filing officer on behalf of statements of
financial interests of agency heads. In this respect, the
Board of Supervisors or its designee - in all probability its
clerk - will have the responsibility of filing officer. See
Government Code §81010, §82027, and §91013.

In response to your fourth question, any employee who
resigns prior to the effective date of a Conflict of Interest
Code which renders the employee a designated employee within
the meaning of the Code is not required to file a statement
of financial interests pursuant to the Code.

Sincerely,

Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
Chief, Conflicts of Interest
Division

*§81005(g) as amended by SB 1373 provides:

(g) All reports and statements required by Chapter
7 -- one original with the agency; which; provided that
for reports and statements filed by the head of the
agency, a member of a board or commission not under a
department of state government or not under the jurisdiction of a local legislative body, and a person who
files a financial disclosure report pursuant to Article
2 (commencing with Section 87200) of Chapter 7, the
agency shall make and retain a copy and forward the
original to the code reviewing body.

Mr. Forest Greber
Deputy County Counsel

- 3 -

August 2, 1976

(continued)

"Code reviewing body" as used in this subdivision
means the commission for the purpose of filing financial disclosure statements prepared pursuant to Article 2 (commencing with Section 87200) of Chapter 7.
"Agency" as used in this subdivision means the clerk
of the court for judges of courts of record.

OFFICE OF THE

Room 702
Administration and Courts Building

COUNTY COUNSEL

Telephone (805) 861-2326

COUNTY OF KERN
1415 Truxtun Avenue
Bakersfield, California
93301

RALPH B. JORDAN
County Counsel

July 1, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

In reply please refer
to Our File Number:

Attn: Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief, Conflicts of Interest Division

Re: Government Code Section 87207

Gentlemen:

It is the view of several attorneys who represent water districts
in this County that the names of persons who pay fees need not be dis-
closed in all cases pursuant to Government Code Section 87207. The
attorneys contend that the disclosure of the names of their clients
would not only violate rules with respect to privilege, but also in-
fringe upon the statutory duty of attorneys under Business and Pro-
fessions Code Section 6068(e) to keep confidential the secrets of
the client.

Conflict of interest codes containing the following provision
have been presented to us:

(g) Contents of Business Entity Reports. When
income of a business entity, including income of a sole
proprietorship, is required to be reported under this
Code, the statement shall contain:

(1) The name, address and a general descrip-
tion of the business activity of the business entity.

(2) In the case of a business entity which
provides legal or brokerage services, the name of
every person from whom the business entity received
payments if the designated employee's distributable
share of such payments from such person was equal to
or greater than One Thousand Dollars ($1,000.00)
during the immediately preceding calendar year and
in the case of other business entities, the name of
every person from whom the business entity received
payments if the designated employee's distributable
share of such payments from such person was equal to
or greater than Ten Thousand Dollars ($10,000.00)
during the immediately preceding calendar year;

Fair Political Practices Commission
Attn: Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
Page 2

provided, however, that such designated employee
need not list the name of every such person or
persons if he files with his statement a declaration
under penalty of perjury stating that to the best of
his knowledge, information and belief all such
persons not listed have neither had nor are likely
to have any business relationship with the District
other than to purchase utility services therefrom
and that no decision made or participated in by such
designated employee by virtue of his position will
have an effect upon any such person not listed,
distinguishable from its effect upon the public
generally.

We would appreciate your opinion as to whether or not the fore-
going provision is in compliance with the Political Reform Act of 1974.

We have been asked by several agencies as to whether or not
the Board of Supervisors will review or monitor the content of dis-
closure statements. We find nothing in the law or regulations which
requires review and would appreciate your opinion.

We have been asked by many directors of special districts and
trustees of school districts if they are required to file disclosure
statements if they resign prior to the effective date of the conflict
of interest codes and would appreciate your views on this matter.

Thank you for your courtesy and cooperation.

Very truly yours,

RALPH B. JORDAN, County Counsel

By [signature]
Forest Greber
Deputy County Counsel

FG:cc

sections.question: The inquiry addresses several questions regarding the content and administration of Conflict of Interest Codes pursuant to Government Code §87300 et seq: (1) Whether client disclosure requirements can be limited under privilege for attorneys; (2) Whether a Conflict of Interest Code section on business entity reports can permit non-disclosure of clients when it is not foreseeable that a client will be materially financially affected by a decision of the designated employee; (3) Whether the Board of Supervisors will review or monitor the content of disclosure statements; (4) Whether an employee who resigns prior to the effective date of a Conflict of Interest Code is required to file a statement of financial interests.
sections.conclusion: First question: The Commission has passed Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18740 which provides for a limited privilege for not disclosing financial interests of attorneys in narrow circumstances. Second question: The Commission specifically disavows language permitting non-disclosure; if it is foreseeable that a client will be materially financially affected by a decision, that client's name must be listed. Third question: Generally no, the Board of Supervisors will not have review or monitoring functions regarding disclosure statement content, but will serve as filing officer for agency heads' statements. Fourth question: Any employee who resigns prior to the effective date is not required to file a statement of financial interests.
sections.question_synthetic: The County Counsel asks four questions regarding conflict of interest code requirements: whether attorneys can limit client disclosure under privilege, whether codes can permit non-disclosure of clients when not materially affected by official decisions, whether the Board of Supervisors reviews disclosure statements, and whether departing employees must file financial disclosures.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that: (1) a new regulation permits limited attorney-client privilege for disclosure; (2) clients must be disclosed when their interests are foreseeably affected by the official's decisions; (3) the Board of Supervisors has no review/monitoring role but serves as filing officer; and (4) employees resigning before the code's effective date are not required to file.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (July 1, 1976 letter from County Counsel) and FPPC response letter (August 2, 1976). Analysis focuses on the FPPC advice letter response. Clear question and conclusion sections identifiable. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not significantly impair understanding.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The inquiry addresses several questions regarding the content and administration of Conflict of Interest Codes pursuant to Government Code §87300 et seq: (1) Whether client disclosure requirements can be limited under privilege for attorneys; (2) Whether a Conflict of Interest Code section on business entity reports can permit non-disclosure of clients when it is not foreseeable that a client will be materially financially affected by a decision of the designated employee; (3) Whether the Board of Supervisors will review or monitor the content of disclosure statements; (4) Whether an employee who resigns prior to the effective date of a Conflict of Interest Code is required to file a statement of financial interests.

CONCLUSION: First question: The Commission has passed Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18740 which provides for a limited privilege for not disclosing financial interests of attorneys in narrow circumstances. Second question: The Commission specifically disavows language permitting non-disclosure; if it is foreseeable that a client will be materially financially affected by a decision, that client's name must be listed. Third question: Generally no, the Board of Supervisors will not have review or monitoring functions regarding disclosure statement content, but will serve as filing officer for agency heads' statements. Fourth question: Any employee who resigns prior to the effective date is not required to file a statement of financial interests.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to multiple questions from Kern County regarding conflict of interest code administration, addressing attorney-client privilege in disclosure, client reporting requirements based on foreseeability of financial impact, board oversight functions, and filing requirements for departing employees.
